text: Upon entering the abdominal cavity, the surgeon encounters severe inflammatory adhesions between the colon and gallbladder. Using monopolar scissors, the surgeon performs adhesiolysis, carefully mobilizing the colon from the fibrotic and sclerotic gallbladder. A plane is successfully opened between the colon and gallbladder, progressing beyond the worst inflammation.

The surgeon further mobilizes the colon from the liver and hepatoduodenal ligament, preparing to close the fistula between the colon and gallbladder. The opening in the colon is closed before proceeding with the cholecystectomy.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the colon, dissecting the borders of the opening. A large opening in the transverse colon is visible, which is manually closed to prevent stenosis.

The surgeon performs a Heineken Mikulić Structuroplasty on the colon using robotic assistance. Two corner stitches with V-Lock II-O are placed, followed by the creation of the inner layer with V-Lock III-O in a running fashion. Barbed sutures are used for continuous traction. After completing the inner layer, the outer layer is formed using previously placed stitches, and the closure is finalized by tying the outer layer stitches.

The surgeon performs a cholecystectomy, encountering difficulty in identifying a clear plane between the gallbladder and the liver. A small wedge resection of liver segments IVb and Vb is executed. The gallbladder is excised and sent for pathological examination.

The surgeon dissects the hepatoduodenal ligament to isolate the common bile duct. The common bile duct is opened anteriorly at the level of the fistula between the gallbladder and the common bile duct. The posterior wall is isolated and transected, allowing extraction of a large stone from the distal part of the common bile duct. The distal common bile duct is isolated from surrounding structures to facilitate closure of the stump.
The surgeon uses a stapler to close the large caliber stump of the bile duct, which is identified as a central pathology in the surgical field.

The surgeon performs a Roux-en-Y hepaticojejunostomy, partially splitting the omentum due to its thickness. The small bowel is transected approximately thirty centimeters distal to the ligament of Treitz.

The surgeon lifts the small bowel in an anti-mesocolic orientation and performs an end-to-side hepatic incision ostomy. A posterior layer is sutured using a V-lock IV-O absorbable wire, starting medially and moving laterally, without opening the small bowel. The barbed suture maintains continuous traction during the running suture of the posterior layer.

The surgeon completes the posterior layer of the anastomosis and creates a small enterotomy. The anterior layer is sutured with a second stitch, starting medially and moving laterally, using the previous suture for traction. The large caliber of the common bile duct ensures no risk of incorporating the posterior layer while suturing the anterior layer. The anastomosis is visually completed.

The surgeon fixes the sutures with a hemolog clip and restores FENST by creating an enteroenterostomy approximately sixty centimeters distal to the hepatic oediginostomy. The posterior layer is sutured using a VLOG-IIO absorbable wire in a running fashion. The small bowel is opened on both sides, and an inner layer is created with a VLOG-O absorbable wire, completed in a running fashion. Finally, another VLOG-O is used for the outer layer on the anterior side of the anastomosis, completing the anastomosis.
